home.insight.didYouKnowText: Research shows grateful people experience 23% lower cortisol — the stress hormone. Shukr isn't just worship, it's medicine.
home.insight.shukrEffectText: What you thank Allah for, He multiplies. What science calls the Law of Attraction, Islam called Shukr 1,400 years ago.

pasco.results.savedDesc: Sign up to track how your profile evolves over time and get AI-powered growth recommendations.
pasco.archetype.The Mujtahid.desc: You approach life with deep analytical rigor and a commitment to justice. You seek truth through evidence, not opinion.
pasco.archetype.The Rahma Shield.desc: Your superpower is compassion combined with emotional depth. People feel safe around you because you lead with mercy.
pasco.archetype.The Architect.desc: You see systems where others see chaos. Strategic, methodical, and forward-thinking — you build things that last.
pasco.archetype.The Bridge Builder.desc: You connect people, ideas, and communities. Your natural communication and collaboration skills make you the glue that holds groups together.
pasco.archetype.The Visionary.desc: You see possibilities before others do. Creative, open-minded, and inspiring — you imagine what could be and work to make it real.
pasco.archetype.The Steady Flame.desc: Your patience and emotional stability are your greatest gifts. In a world of noise, you are the calm that others gravitate toward.
pasco.archetype.The Catalyst.desc: You lead from the front with energy and conviction. People follow you not because you demand it, but because your passion is contagious.
pasco.archetype.The Sage.desc: You learn fast, see patterns others miss, and synthesize information with rare clarity. People come to you when they need understanding.
pasco.archetype.The Guardian.desc: Integrity and stewardship define you. You protect what matters — trust, resources, relationships — with quiet, unwavering commitment.
pasco.archetype.The Healer.desc: You carry a rare gift: the ability to sit with people in their pain and help them find light. Emotional intelligence meets deep compassion.
pasco.archetype.The Shukr Warrior.desc: Gratitude isn't just a practice for you — it's a lens. You find blessings in difficulty and turn every experience into growth.
pasco.archetype.The Ummah Builder.desc: You think in terms of community, legacy, and collective impact. Your leadership comes through service, not status.
pasco.q1.title: The New Masjid
pasco.q1.scenario: You've just moved to a new city and visit the local masjid for the first time. After salah, people are mingling in the lobby and you don't know a single person. The chai is being passed around and someone glances your way.
pasco.q1.a: You walk up to the nearest group, introduce yourself, and ask how long they've been coming here.
pasco.q1.b: You find a quiet corner, take in the vibe, and make mental notes about the community before deciding if this is your place.
pasco.q1.c: You notice the uncle struggling to stack chairs and jump in to help — that's your way in.
pasco.q1.d: You go home, look up the masjid's events calendar and social media, and come back next week with a plan to join a specific program.
pasco.q2.title: Ramadan Iftar Politics
pasco.q2.scenario: It's the second week of Ramadan and your family is hosting a big iftar. Your aunt starts making comments about how "young people these days don't take Ramadan seriously" — clearly aimed at your cousin who's sitting right there looking uncomfortable.
pasco.q2.a: You crack a light joke to change the energy and steer the conversation toward something everyone can enjoy.
pasco.q2.b: You gently acknowledge your aunt's concern while also checking in on your cousin later privately.
pasco.q2.c: You respectfully push back: "Auntie, everyone's Ramadan journey is different" — someone needs to say it.
pasco.q2.d: You stay quiet during dinner but reflect on it deeply afterward — maybe even journal about it.
pasco.q3.title: Career vs. Family
pasco.q3.scenario: Your parents have always envisioned you in a "stable" career — medicine, engineering, law. But you've discovered a passion for something completely different: maybe it's art, entrepreneurship, Islamic psychology, or content creation. A family gathering is coming up and you know the question is coming.
pasco.q3.a: You prepare a thoughtful case showing how your path aligns with Islamic values of excellence and contribution, then present it to your parents before the gathering.
pasco.q3.b: You own it fully. If it comes up at the gathering, you speak about it with genuine passion and let the excitement do the convincing.
pasco.q3.c: You keep working on your passion quietly, building results that will speak for themselves. Actions over arguments.
pasco.q3.d: You consult a mentor or someone your parents respect, hoping they can help bridge the gap between generations.
pasco.q4.title: Eid Morning Energy
pasco.q4.scenario: It's Eid morning. The prayer is done, everyone's dressed up, and the day stretches ahead. Your group chat is blowing up with plans — some people want a big gathering, others want something chill. You have complete freedom to shape your day.
pasco.q4.a: You're bouncing between three Eid gatherings, hugging everyone, taking photos, and making sure no one's left out.
pasco.q4.b: You organize a small, intentional gathering with your closest people — good food, good conversation, maybe a meaningful activity.
pasco.q4.c: You do something spontaneous — maybe drive somewhere new, try a restaurant you've never been to, or just see where the day takes you.
pasco.q4.d: You spend the morning with family, then carve out solo time to reflect on the month that just passed and set intentions.
pasco.q5.title: The Interfaith Moment
pasco.q5.scenario: A non-Muslim colleague or classmate genuinely asks you: "What's it actually like being Muslim? I feel like I only hear one narrative." It's not hostile — they're sincerely curious. You're the only Muslim they know well.
pasco.q5.a: You share personal stories — your favorite Ramadan memories, what Jummah feels like, the beauty you experience. You make it human and real.
pasco.q5.b: You break it down clearly: here's what we believe, here's what we practice, here's what the media gets wrong. Structured and informative.
pasco.q5.c: You ask them what they've heard so far, what sparked the curiosity, and then address their specific gaps. It's a dialogue, not a lecture.
pasco.q5.d: You say "Why don't you come see for yourself?" and invite them to an iftar, a community event, or just a casual hangout with your Muslim friends.
pasco.q6.title: Budget Crisis
pasco.q6.scenario: Your masjid's annual community dinner is two weeks away. The budget just got slashed in half because a major donor pulled out. The venue is booked, invites are sent, 200 people are coming. The committee is panicking.
pasco.q6.a: You pull up the full budget spreadsheet, identify every line item, and figure out exactly what can be cut without ruining the experience.
pasco.q6.b: You propose turning it into a potluck-style event, get local Muslim businesses to sponsor dishes, and turn the constraint into a community-building moment.
pasco.q6.c: You immediately start making calls — there's always someone in the community who can help. You personally reach out to 10 potential donors before the day is over.
pasco.q6.d: You point out this happens every year and suggest the committee build a reserve fund and diversify donors. You're already thinking about next year.
pasco.q7.title: Scholar Disagreement
pasco.q7.scenario: You're researching an Islamic topic that matters to you — maybe it's about finances, relationships, or daily practice. You find that respected scholars have completely opposite opinions, and both sides have strong evidence. People in your circle are getting heated about it.
pasco.q7.a: You go to the primary sources yourself. You want to understand the reasoning behind each position, not just the conclusion.
pasco.q7.b: You look for the underlying principles both sides agree on and form your own nuanced position that takes the best from each.
pasco.q7.c: You identify which opinion applies best to your specific life situation and context, and go with that. Scholarly disagreement is a mercy.
pasco.q7.d: You find the most trusted scholar you follow, understand their reasoning well enough to be at peace with it, and move on.
pasco.q8.title: Halal Business Dilemma
pasco.q8.scenario: You've been offered a business opportunity or job that pays extremely well. The work itself is halal, but the company's broader portfolio includes some questionable areas. It's not clearly haram, but it's not clearly clean either. You need the money.
pasco.q8.a: You dig into the company's full revenue breakdown, consult with someone knowledgeable, and make a decision based on the actual numbers and principles.
pasco.q8.b: You explore whether you can negotiate your role to be distanced from the questionable areas, or propose an alternative arrangement.
pasco.q8.c: You weigh this against your 5-year plan. Is this a stepping stone or a trap? You think about where this path leads, not just where it starts.
pasco.q8.d: You quickly educate yourself on the Islamic frameworks for financial grey areas and make an informed call fast.
pasco.q9.title: Information Overload
pasco.q9.scenario: Your social media feed is flooded with devastating news about Muslims suffering somewhere in the world. Everyone's sharing posts, opinions are flying, fundraisers are popping up, and you're feeling the weight of it. Some information contradicts other information.
pasco.q9.a: You stop scrolling, find 2-3 verified sources, cross-reference the claims, and only then form an opinion or take action.
pasco.q9.b: You step back from the noise and try to understand the bigger picture: historical context, geopolitical patterns, what's actually new.
pasco.q9.c: You pick ONE concrete thing you can do right now — donate, volunteer, organize — and channel the overwhelm into impact.
pasco.q9.d: You use this as a catalyst to deeply learn about the issue, its roots, and what sustainable help looks like.
pasco.q10.title: New Skill for Deen
pasco.q10.scenario: You decide you want to learn something new that would benefit your deen and your community — maybe it's Arabic, Islamic finance, counseling skills, or Quran memorization. You've tried before and didn't stick with it.
pasco.q10.a: You research the best method, create a structured schedule, set milestones, and track your progress. If the system is right, the results will follow.
pasco.q10.b: You analyze why you failed before, identify the exact friction points, and design around them.
pasco.q10.c: You throw yourself in: join a class, find a study partner, change your phone language. Total environment shift.
pasco.q10.d: You make it enjoyable: gamify it, connect it to something you already love, or create content about it as you learn.
pasco.q11.title: Leading the Halaqah
pasco.q11.scenario: You've been asked to lead a weekly halaqah at your masjid. The group is mixed — some people know a lot, some are brand new to practicing, and a few are just there because their friends dragged them. Your first session is tomorrow.
pasco.q11.a: You prepare a topic but plan to make it discussion-based. Your job is to create space where everyone feels heard, especially the quiet ones.
pasco.q11.b: You craft a compelling opening that hooks everyone regardless of their level. Stories, analogies, real-life examples.
pasco.q11.c: You design a structured 8-week curriculum with progressive topics. You share the roadmap upfront so everyone sees the journey.
pasco.q11.d: You start by asking the group what they actually need. What questions keep them up at night? You build from their reality.
pasco.q12.title: Giving Nasiha
pasco.q12.scenario: A close friend is making a decision you genuinely believe is harmful for them — maybe it's a relationship, a financial choice, or a lifestyle pattern. They haven't asked for your opinion. You care about them deeply and you can see where this is heading.
pasco.q12.a: You find a private, relaxed moment and share your concern with genuine love. You focus on how you feel, not what they're doing wrong.
pasco.q12.b: You ask thoughtful questions that help them see the issue themselves: "Have you thought about...?" You guide without telling.
pasco.q12.c: You don't say anything directly but share your own similar experience or subtly model the better choice in your own life.
pasco.q12.d: You tell them straight: "I love you, and I have to be honest with you." You'd rather risk awkwardness now than watch them suffer later.
pasco.q13.title: Community Conflict
pasco.q13.scenario: Two groups in your masjid community are in a heated disagreement — maybe it's about how to run an event, a policy change, or a leadership decision. Both sides feel strongly and it's affecting the community vibe. Someone asks you to help mediate.
pasco.q13.a: You meet with each side separately first, listen deeply, identify the real needs beneath the positions, then bring them together.
pasco.q13.b: You draft a clear, neutral summary of both positions and share it. Transparency and accurate framing can de-escalate most conflicts.
pasco.q13.c: You get both sides working on a small shared project together. People who serve together fight less. Action heals what arguments can't.
pasco.q13.d: You suggest implementing a structured decision-making process: clear criteria, transparent process. Remove the emotion, focus on the system.
pasco.q14.title: The Multicultural Masjid
pasco.q14.scenario: Your community has Muslims from five different cultural backgrounds. There's growing tension because one culture tends to dominate the masjid's events, food, and leadership style. Others feel sidelined. You've been asked to help plan the next big community event.
pasco.q14.a: You create a committee with at least one person from each cultural group and give each real decision-making power.
pasco.q14.b: You design the event around sharing: each culture gets a segment to showcase their food, art, or tradition. Turn diversity into the program.
pasco.q14.c: You have private conversations with key people from each group, understand their specific frustrations, and address those individually.
pasco.q14.d: You propose a rotating leadership model for all future events and draft clear guidelines for inclusive planning. Fix the system.
pasco.q15.title: The Faith Questioner
pasco.q15.scenario: Someone you care about — a sibling, friend, or community member — confides that they're struggling with their faith. They're not hostile toward Islam; they're just lost, confused, and maybe a little ashamed to admit it. They chose to tell you.
pasco.q15.a: You listen without any judgment or urgency to "fix" them. You validate their courage in speaking up and make sure they know this conversation is safe.
pasco.q15.b: You share resources that helped you or others: a book, a podcast, a scholar who speaks to doubts with intelligence.
pasco.q15.c: You offer to walk with them. Attend a class together, read something, have regular check-ins. They shouldn't do this alone.
pasco.q15.d: You share your own struggles honestly. You let them know that doubt and confusion aren't failure — they're part of the journey.
pasco.q16.title: The Unseen Good Deed
pasco.q16.scenario: You quietly helped someone in a significant way — maybe you paid off someone's grocery bill, mentored a struggling student for months, or supported a family going through hardship. No one knows it was you. Then a situation comes up where getting credit for it would genuinely benefit you.
pasco.q16.a: You keep it to yourself. The deed was between you and Allah. Using it for social capital would ruin it for you spiritually.
pasco.q16.b: You reflect on how you were even in a position to help. The ability to give was itself a blessing you didn't earn.
pasco.q16.c: You'd mention it only if it directly helps the cause — like proving capability for a community role. It's about effectiveness, not ego.
pasco.q16.d: You share it honestly when relevant. Good deeds can inspire others to do the same. Visible virtue can be contagious.
pasco.q17.title: The Shattered Plan
pasco.q17.scenario: You spent months working toward something important — a job, a project, a relationship, a goal you prayed about consistently. It falls through completely, and not because of anything you did wrong. The door just closed. People around you are sympathetic but you're sitting alone with it.
pasco.q17.a: You grieve it, but you hold firm. What's written for you won't miss you, and what missed you was never yours. It hurts, but you trust the process.
pasco.q17.b: Even in the disappointment, you actively look for what you gained: the skills, the people you met, the growth. The outcome failed but the journey didn't.
pasco.q17.c: You give yourself a day or two, then start planning the next move. If this door closed, there's a reason another one needs your energy.
pasco.q17.d: You reach out to people you trust. You process it by talking, being around people who get it. You don't do pain alone.
pasco.q18.title: The Wealth Test
pasco.q18.scenario: You receive a significant amount of money unexpectedly — an inheritance, a bonus, or a business windfall. It's more than you need. You've already fulfilled your zakat obligation. The money is sitting there, and you're thinking about what comes next.
pasco.q18.a: You immediately think about who needs it. You give beyond obligation because you know wealth is a test and you'd rather be on the safe side.
pasco.q18.b: You invest it wisely in halal avenues. Wealth that grows ethically means you can give more in the long run. Stewardship is worship.
pasco.q18.c: You divide it thoughtfully: family obligations, savings, sadaqah, personal needs. Every dollar has a purpose.
pasco.q18.d: You don't rush. You make istikharah, sit with it, and let clarity come. Big money decisions made in excitement or guilt rarely end well.
pasco.q19.title: Justice vs. Mercy
pasco.q19.scenario: Someone in your community — someone you personally like — is caught in a clear ethical violation. Maybe they mismanaged community funds, lied about something significant, or betrayed someone's trust. The community is divided: some want accountability, others want to forgive and move on.
pasco.q19.a: There must be accountability. Not out of anger, but because trust is the foundation of any community. Mercy without justice enables the next violation.
pasco.q19.b: People make mistakes. If they're genuinely remorseful, the community should help them recover. Public humiliation isn't Islamic.
pasco.q19.c: You advocate for a private, structured process: the person acknowledges it, makes amends, and there are clear boundaries. Justice AND mercy, sequenced.
pasco.q19.d: You focus on the system that allowed it. Put checks and balances in place so it can't happen again, regardless of what happens to this person.
pasco.q20.title: The Mirror
pasco.q20.scenario: Late at night, in a quiet moment, you're being completely honest with yourself. No performance, no audience. You think about the gap between who you present to the world and who you actually are. The private you — the one who knows your real struggles, your real intentions, your real fears.
pasco.q20.a: You sit with the gap and take it seriously. You make a specific, actionable change — even a small one. Self-accounting without action is just guilt.
pasco.q20.b: You acknowledge the gap with gentleness. You're human. Allah sees your struggles and still provides for you. The gap is where growth lives.
pasco.q20.c: You identify the one area where the gap is widest and build a plan to close it. Not everything at once — just the one thing that would move the needle most.
pasco.q20.d: You share your struggle with someone you trust — a spouse, a close friend, a mentor. Bringing the private into a safe relationship is how you stop carrying it alone.

gratitude.title: Log Your Shukr
gratitude.desc: What are you grateful for today? Be creative, be deep. Your entry is 100% private.
gratitude.voiceSwitchHint: Switch to Type mode to log your gratitude.
gratitude.aiScoring: AI is scoring creativity, depth & perspective
gratitude.score.title: Creativity Score
gratitude.score.tipProfound: MashaAllah, a truly profound reflection. Keep exploring these deeper layers of gratitude.
gratitude.score.tipThoughtful: Beautiful entry. Try going one layer deeper — what makes this blessing uniquely meaningful to you?
gratitude.score.tipCommon: Good start! Next time, try to be more specific — what exactly about this are you grateful for?
decomp.systemDesc: The invisible systems that enable this
decomp.hookLine: You think this is just 1 blessing? Look deeper...
decomp.revealLine: You thought it was 1 blessing. It was
decomp.quranVerse: And if you should count the favors of Allah, you could not enumerate them.
reflection.title: Reflection Score
reflection.trendEmpty: Log blessings over multiple days to see your trend
reflection.tip.uniqueness.strength: You notice blessings others overlook. Your entries reveal uncommon awareness.
reflection.tip.uniqueness.growth: Look for micro-moments — the way morning light hits your desk, a stranger who held the door. Uncommon blessings score highest.
reflection.tip.depth.strength: Your reflections go beyond surface-level. You naturally ask "why does this matter?"
reflection.tip.depth.growth: Go beyond naming. Ask: What did this teach me? How did it change me? A blessing with a lesson scores 2× higher.
reflection.tip.specificity.strength: You name exact people, moments, and feelings. This precision makes your gratitude powerful.
reflection.tip.specificity.growth: "My mother's dua at Fajr" beats "family support" every time. Name the exact person, exact moment, exact feeling.
reflection.tip.perspective.strength: You see blessings in difficulty. Reframing struggles is the highest level of gratitude.
reflection.tip.perspective.growth: Try: "I'm grateful for the failure that led to..." Reframing struggles as blessings unlocks this dimension.
levels.title: Level Progression
levels.1.name: Shukr Practitioner
levels.1.desc: Build the habit of daily gratitude and prostration of thanks.
levels.1.task.0: Log 3 gratitudes daily for 30 consecutive days
levels.1.task.1: Perform 1 Sajdah al-Shukr (prostration of thanks) daily
levels.2.name: Shukr + Charity
levels.2.desc: Combine gratitude with generosity. Give back while you count blessings.
levels.2.task.1: Complete 3 acts of charity this month
levels.3.name: Tahajjud Gratitude
levels.3.desc: Rise for the night prayer (Tahajjud) and combine it with gratitude journaling.
levels.3.task.0: Log gratitude during Tahajjud for 30 days
levels.4.name: Shukr Master
levels.4.desc: Complete mastery: gratitude becomes second nature. Mentor others and lead communities.
levels.4.reward: DinarZ + "Shukr Master" Badge + Community Leader Status
